content.text: Why Standard Agile-at-Scale Needs Adaptation for GenAI

SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale were all designed around software engineering work where the cost of an iteration is a sprint's worth of engineering and the output is testable code. GenAI programmes introduce a different kind of work:
that may take days to run, produce probabilistic outputs rather than pass/fail tests, and have costs that scale with compute rather than team size.

The core tension: agile-at-scale frameworks optimise for regular, predictable delivery cadences. ML experimentation is inherently irregular — a fine-tuning run might complete in 4 hours or 4 days depending on dataset size and compute availability.
Rather than abandoning the chosen framework, the pragmatic approach is to apply it for the
while defining explicit adaptations for the ML research track. The two tracks run in parallel, synchronise at defined integration points, and feed into a shared programme increment or release.

attrs.alt: Comparison of SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale for GenAI programmes showing best-fit scenarios, GenAI-specific risks, required adaptations, and overall fit ratings
attrs.title: Agile-at-Scale Frameworks GenAI Fit

content.text: LeSS tends to be the best natural fit for GenAI programmes at the 2–8 team scale because its single product backlog forces explicit prioritisation between ML research items and product features, and its feature-team model accommodates the cross-functional work that GenAI requires (data engineering, ML, product, platform all contributing to the same user-facing outcome). SAFe works well at enterprise scale but requires deliberate adaptations to avoid the PI cadence becoming a bottleneck for fast-moving model development.

SAFe's Program Increment is typically 10 weeks with a fixed scope committed at PI Planning. This works poorly when a key dependency is a model evaluation result that is not known until week 6. Two adaptations prevent the PI cadence from blocking ML progress:

: designate a separate capacity allocation (typically 20–30% of the ML team's capacity) for model experiments that operate outside the PI commitment. Spikes feed into future PI planning as input rather than being committed items. This prevents the PI objective from being hostage to experiment outcomes.
Model eval milestone as PI boundary

: schedule the PI boundary to coincide with a planned model evaluation checkpoint. The eval result informs the next PI's scope — effectively using the PI cadence to create a structured decision point rather than an arbitrary time boundary.

LeSS's single product backlog becomes complex when ML research items and product feature items sit alongside each other — they have very different cost structures, uncertainty profiles, and definition-of-done criteria. The recommended adaptation is a
: standard user stories, features, and technical debt items. Estimated in story points. Owned by the product PO.

: time-boxed spikes with a clearly stated hypothesis, success criterion, and compute budget. Estimated in days of ML engineer time + compute cost (not story points). Owned by a technical PO (ML Lead or Head of AI Engineering).
Regardless of which framework is used, GenAI programmes benefit from an

— a community of practice that cuts across all delivery teams and owns ML-specific standards, tooling, and knowledge sharing. The guild is not a team; it has no delivery commitments. It is the mechanism through which ML best practices propagate across the programme.
Compute budget review; forecast vs actuals

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class GuildCadence(Enum):
    PER_EXPERIMENT = "per_experiment"
    BIWEEKLY       = "biweekly"
    MONTHLY        = "monthly"
    QUARTERLY      = "quarterly"
    PER_RELEASE    = "per_release"

@dataclass
class GuildResponsibility:
    name: str
    description: str
    cadence: GuildCadence
    owner_role: str       # who in the guild is accountable
    output: str

@dataclass
class MLGuild:
    programme: str
    members: list[str]   # team names whose ML engineers participate
    lead: str
    responsibilities: list[GuildResponsibility] = field(default_factory=list)

    def responsibilities_by_cadence(
        self, cadence: GuildCadence
    ) -> list[GuildResponsibility]:
        return [r for r in self.responsibilities if r.cadence == cadence]

    def experiment_gate_checklist(self) -> list[str]:
        """
        Standard checklist applied by the guild before any ML experiment
        is added to the backlog.
        """
        return [
            "Hypothesis is falsifiable and stated in measurable terms",
            "Success criterion is binary (pass/fail), not subjective",
            "Compute budget is approved by tech lead",
            "Time-box is set; experiment will be terminated if not complete by deadline",
            "Data access and governance requirements are met",
            "Safety implications of the experiment have been considered",
            "Results will be shared at next guild session regardless of outcome",
        ]
The selection should be driven by team size, existing adoption, and the programme's primary delivery risk:
Minimal overhead; single backlog forces prioritisation
Strong governance; PI cadence suits compliance checkpoints
Organic scaling; easy to adopt incrementally
Dual-lane backlog accommodates high experiment volume
SAFe's PI Planning is a 2-day event where teams commit to PI objectives. For GenAI programmes, the standard PI planning process needs three adaptations to function well:

: 30 minutes before the main PI planning event, the ML team presents results from spikes completed in the previous PI. This gives product and platform teams the data they need to set realistic committed objectives. Without this, PI planning proceeds with uncertain ML assumptions and teams over-commit.

: SAFe's standard confidence vote (fist-of-five) is applied to both committed and spike objectives, but with different thresholds. Committed objectives need ≥3 (average) confidence. Spike objectives need ≥2 (teams accept the experiment may fail, but must be confident the spike will produce a clear result).
Compute cost as a PI resource

: include GPU/TPU compute budget alongside headcount in PI resource planning. Teams allocate compute hours per PI alongside engineering capacity, preventing surprise budget overruns mid-PI when a long training run is triggered.
Sprint Review and System Demo Adaptations

Standard LeSS and SAFe sprint reviews demonstrate working software. For GenAI programmes, working software and working models are two different things — a model might improve on an eval benchmark but the user-facing feature is not yet integrated. The sprint review needs to handle both:
: standard product increment demo. The product is in a deployable state. Stakeholders can use it.

: model evaluation results, experiment readouts, quality metrics. Not always a demo — sometimes it is a graph showing eval score progression over the sprint. The audience is technical leads and the product PO; executives join only if there is a significant result.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class SprintReviewAgenda:
    sprint_number: int
    review_date: date
    software_demos: list[str]        # feature or story titles to demo
    ml_progress_updates: list[str]   # experiment results, eval scores
    integration_point: bool          # True if this is a cross-team integration sprint
    decisions_needed: list[str]      # go/no-go items for stakeholders
    time_box_minutes: int = 60

    def ml_time_allocation_minutes(self) -> int:
        """
        Allocate ML time proportional to the number of ML updates.
        Cap at 40% of total time so the software demo is never crowded out.
        """
        total_items = len(self.software_demos) + len(self.ml_progress_updates)
        if not total_items:
            return 0
        ml_proportion = len(self.ml_progress_updates) / total_items
        return int(min(0.4, ml_proportion) * self.time_box_minutes)

    def software_time_allocation_minutes(self) -> int:
        return self.time_box_minutes - self.ml_time_allocation_minutes() - 10  # 10 min for decisions

    def format_agenda(self) -> str:
        ml_time = self.ml_time_allocation_minutes()
        sw_time = self.software_time_allocation_minutes()
        lines = [
            f"Sprint {self.sprint_number} Review — {self.review_date} ({self.time_box_minutes} min)",
            f"  [0-{sw_time}min] Software demos: {', '.join(self.software_demos) or 'none'}",
            f"  [{sw_time}-{sw_time+ml_time}min] ML progress: {', '.join(self.ml_progress_updates) or 'none'}",
            f"  [{sw_time+ml_time}-{self.time_box_minutes}min] Decisions: {', '.join(self.decisions_needed) or 'none'}",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)


# Example
agenda = SprintReviewAgenda(
    sprint_number=7,
    review_date=date(2025, 3, 14),
    software_demos=["AI suggestion panel v2", "Agent handoff flow"],
    ml_progress_updates=["Fine-tune run: eval 84% (+3%)", "RAG latency: 210ms p99 (-15%)"],
    integration_point=False,
    decisions_needed=["Approve go to system test if eval ≥ 85%?"],
)
print(agenda.format_agenda())

For integration sprints (where all teams converge), the System Demo is the mechanism. All teams demonstrate their increments together in a shared environment. For GenAI programmes, this is the moment where the model, the platform, the data pipeline, and the product UX are validated together for the first time — make it a formal event with explicit go/no-go criteria prepared in advance.
Common Anti-Patterns When Applying Agile-at-Scale to GenAI
Treating model fine-tuning as a story

: fine-tuning a model is not a story with a definition of done that can be moved to 'Done' at sprint end. It is an experiment. Put it in the experiment lane, not the product backlog, and judge it on hypothesis result, not completion.

: story points are meaningless for ML experiments. Tracking velocity for the ML track as if it were a product track creates pressure to estimate experiments in story points, which produces arbitrary numbers and misleads the PM. Track ML work in experiment count + outcomes, not velocity.
PI boundary forcing a premature commitment on model quality

: if the PI ends before model evaluation is complete, teams are forced to commit to a next-PI product scope they cannot validate. Use the pre-PI experiment readout (see above) or extend the spike window into the early weeks of the next PI before committing.
Single PO for both ML and product

: the product PO optimises for user value; the ML track needs a technical PO (typically the ML Lead) who can judge whether a 2% eval improvement justifies another sprint of fine-tuning. Combining the role into one person almost always results in one of the tracks being systematically underserved.

SAFe, LeSS, and Scrum@Scale each apply to GenAI programmes with different trade-offs. LeSS is the most natural fit at mid-scale because its simplicity and single product backlog accommodate the ML research track without excessive ceremony. SAFe works at enterprise scale but requires explicit adaptations — an ML spike track and model eval checkpoints aligned to PI boundaries — to prevent the PI cadence from gating experimental work. Across all frameworks, an

provides the cross-team governance layer that standard agile frameworks do not natively include: experiment design review, compute cost governance, and safety sign-off. The guild is the connective tissue between agile delivery rhythm and ML research discipline.
